mcqs.question: Which relational algebra operator is used to retrieve rows that satisfy a specific condition?

options.text: Projection (π)
Selection (σ)
Union (∪)
Rename (ρ)

mcqs.explanation: Selection (σ) filters rows from a relation based on a predicate, returning only those that satisfy the condition.

mcqs.question: What is the effect of applying the projection operator (π) on a relation?

options.text: It returns all distinct tuples of the relation
It eliminates duplicate tuples in the result
It returns only the specified columns (attributes) of the relation
It merges two relations into one

mcqs.explanation: Projection keeps only the chosen attributes, discarding all others, and (by default) removes duplicate rows.

mcqs.question: Given relations R(A,B) = {(1,2),(3,4)} and S(A,B) = {(3,4),(5,6)}, what is the result of the set‑difference R - S?
mcqs.explanation: Set difference returns tuples that belong to R but not to S; only (1,2) satisfies this.

mcqs.question: If relation R has 5 tuples and relation S has 7 tuples, how many tuples will the Cartesian product R × S contain?
mcqs.explanation: Cartesian product concatenates every tuple of R with every tuple of S, yielding the product of their cardinalities (5×7 = 35).

mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of the rename operator (ρ) in relational algebra?

options.text: To change attribute names to avoid ambiguity in composite queries
To delete duplicate tuples
To combine two relations
To filter rows based on a condition

mcqs.explanation: Rename assigns a new relation name or new attribute names, facilitating operations such as self‑joins where name clashes could occur.

mcqs.question: Which statement correctly describes the relationship between natural join and theta join?

options.text: Natural join is a special case of theta join where the condition is equality on all common attributes
Theta join always eliminates duplicate attributes
Natural join requires an explicit join condition
Theta join cannot be expressed using selection and Cartesian product

mcqs.explanation: Natural join implicitly uses equality predicates on all shared attribute names, which is equivalent to a theta join with those equality conditions.

mcqs.question: Given Enrolled(StudentID, CourseID) and Course(CourseID, Instructor), which relational‑algebra expression returns the IDs of students who have enrolled in ALL courses taught by Instructor 'Jain'?

options.text: π_StudentID(Enrolled) ÷ π_CourseID(σ_Instructor='Jain'(Course))
π_StudentID(Enrolled) - (π_StudentID(Enrolled) × π_CourseID(σ_Instructor='Jain'(Course)) - Enrolled)
(π_StudentID(Enrolled) × π_CourseID(σ_Instructor='Jain'(Course))) - Enrolled
None of the above

mcqs.explanation: The division operator returns those StudentIDs that are related to every CourseID in the set of courses taught by Jain.

mcqs.question: Consider the expressions E1 = σ_{A>5}(R) ⋈_{R.B=S.B} S and E2 = (σ_{A>5}(R)) ⋈ S, where R and S share attribute B. Which statement is correct?

options.text: E1 and E2 are always equivalent
E1 is equivalent to E2 only if the selection condition is on attributes of R only
E2 always returns fewer tuples than E1
E1 can be transformed to E2 using commutativity of selection over join

mcqs.explanation: Because the selection σ_{A>5} involves only attribute A from R, it can be pushed into R before the join, making E1 equivalent to E2 only under that condition.
